name: Qilin
description: The Qilin (麒麟), sometimes referred to as the Chinese unicorn, is a mythical hooved chimerical creature that appears in Chinese mythology. It is often depicted with a single horn, though variations exist. It is a symbol of auspiciousness, prosperity, and serenity, and is said to appear with the imminent arrival or passing of a sage or illustrious ruler. The Qilin is considered one of the four noble animals, alongside the dragon, phoenix, and tortoise, and is believed to possess a gentle and benevolent nature. Unlike dragons, which can be capricious, the Qilin is exclusively associated with good omens and justice. Its appearance is often described as a composite of several animals, including a deer-like body, an ox's tail, and dragon-like scales. The Qilin is a powerful symbol of good fortune and is often depicted in art and architecture, representing peace and prosperity.
subtitle: Auspicious Chinese chimera
appearance: The Qilin typically has the body of a deer, the tail of an ox, scales of a dragon, and sometimes a single horn, though depictions vary. Its coat is often multicolored, with vibrant hues of gold, red, and blue. It can also be depicted with a flowing mane and beard, adding to its majestic appearance. Some depictions give it cloven hooves, while others show it with dragon-like claws. The Qilin's eyes are often described as being filled with wisdom and compassion.
habitat: Qilin are said to appear in places of peace and prosperity, often near virtuous rulers or in areas of natural beauty. They are not tied to a specific geographic location, but rather manifest wherever their auspicious presence is needed.
behavior: Qilin are gentle and benevolent creatures, never harming living beings. They are said to be vegetarians, only consuming vegetation and avoiding trampling on insects. They are also believed to be wise and discerning, able to recognize virtue and wickedness. Their appearance is often a sign of impending good fortune or the arrival of a great leader.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Confucius
keyMyths.description: Legend says that a Qilin appeared to the mother of Confucius before his birth, carrying a jade tablet inscribed with the prophecy of his greatness. This event solidified the Qilin's association with wisdom and the birth of exceptional individuals. Its appearance foreshadowed the immense impact Confucius would have on Chinese society and philosophy.
keyMyths.source: Records of the Grand Historian by Sima Qian

keyMyths.title: Qilin and the Yellow Emperor
keyMyths.description: During the reign of the Yellow Emperor, a Qilin is said to have appeared, bringing with it a message of peace and prosperity. This event is often cited as an example of the Qilin's role as a harbinger of good fortune and a symbol of harmonious rule. The Qilin validated the Emperor's virtuous reign.

keyMyths.title: The Hunt of the Qilin
keyMyths.description: In some stories, the Qilin is hunted, but never successfully captured. Its elusiveness and inherent goodness make it impossible for those with impure intentions to harm it. The act of attempting to harm a Qilin is seen as a grave offense, bringing misfortune upon the perpetrator and their community. This reinforces the Qilin's status as a protected and revered creature.

sources.author: Visser, M.W. de
sources.work: The Dragon in China and Japan
sources.description: A comprehensive study on the symbolism of dragons and related creatures in East Asian cultures, including detailed information on the Qilin.
sources.text: A comprehensive study on the symbolism of dragons and related creatures in East Asian cultures, including detailed information on the Qilin.

sources.author: Williams, C.A.S.
sources.work: Outlines of Chinese Symbolism and Art Motives
sources.description: A valuable resource for understanding the symbolic meanings associated with various Chinese motifs, including the Qilin and its auspicious significance.
sources.text: A valuable resource for understanding the symbolic meanings associated with various Chinese motifs, including the Qilin and its auspicious significance.

sources.author: Eberhard, Wolfram
sources.work: Dictionary of Chinese Symbols: Hidden Symbols in Chinese Life and Thought
sources.description: Provides a detailed examination of the Qilin's role as a symbol of prosperity, good fortune, and wise leadership in Chinese culture.
sources.text: Provides a detailed examination of the Qilin's role as a symbol of prosperity, good fortune, and wise leadership in Chinese culture.

sources.author: Strassberg, Richard E.
sources.work: A Chinese Bestiary: Strange Creatures from the Guideways Through Mountains and Seas
sources.description: Explores the early depictions and evolution of mythical creatures in Chinese literature, including the Qilin and its various forms and attributes.
sources.text: Explores the early depictions and evolution of mythical creatures in Chinese literature, including the Qilin and its various forms and attributes.
